summary: Spolsky calls on web application developers to start demanding browser features that support rich application development, now that graphic designers have gotten their CSS wish list fulfilled. He outlines a detailed list of features he wants to see in browsers, including inline editing, fast asynchronous server queries, standard UI controls, compiled JavaScript, and richer event handling. He explicitly rejects proprietary plugin-based solutions like Java Applets and Macromedia, insisting that improvements must be deeply integrated with DHTML and CSS. He also demands graceful degradation for legacy browsers and dismisses schemes requiring mass user adoption of new plugins. The post is remarkably prescient, essentially describing what would become AJAX, HTML5, and modern web APIs years before they materialized. Spolsky frames this as the application developers' turn to shape the web platform after designers had their era.
key_points: Web platform evolution has served designers (CSS) but neglected application developers who need richer interaction primitives
Browsers need fast asynchronous server communication to enable responsive UIs — essentially describing AJAX before the term existed
Standard UI controls (menus, toolbars, tree views, dialogs) should be built into browsers rather than reimplemented inconsistently by every site
Solutions must integrate with the open web stack (HTML/CSS/JS) rather than relying on proprietary plugins like Flash or Java Applets
Browser development stagnation in the late 90s — caused by Netscape's rewrite and Microsoft's neglect of IE — delayed these capabilities
money_quotes: It's time for us application developers to start clamoring for the features we need to develop great web applications.

It should be possible to have a 300,000 employee directory on the server and create a web app that has a list box where you can type the first few letters of an employee's name and see a filtered list as fast as you can type on the screen.
I want this stuff integrated with DHTML and CSS, deeply in the fabric of the web.
These things would have happened if browser development hadn't ground to a halt in the late 90s due to the misguided Netscape-rewrite-project and the lock-IE-developers-in-a-dungeon project.
Boil the ocean schemes that require 400,000,000 users to install some thingamajig before you get anything useful. Such schemes will not go anywhere.
It's time to make Microsoft play catchup again. Fire and Motion Baby.
It's time for application developers to start clamoring for the next generation of the Web now that the graphic designers got their wish list taken care of.
key_insight: The web platform needed to evolve from a document-presentation medium into a rich application platform through native browser capabilities, not proprietary plugins — a vision that anticipated AJAX, HTML5, and modern web development by several years.
